sections.content:
You stayed late on the deck, and your boss called it 'brilliant' in front of the team. The warmth of that word carried you for days.

So you chased it again. And again. Slowly you notice your choices bending — toward the visible work that gets noticed, away from the quiet work that matters. You're not asking 'is this right?' anymore. You're asking 'will this get me that feeling again?' Somewhere along the way, the applause stopped being a reward for the work and became the reason for it.

sections.title: The Story
sections.content:
There's an old collection of Indian animal fables called the Panchatantra, written to teach wisdom through simple stories. One of the most famous is about a monkey and a crocodile.

A monkey lives in a fruit tree by a river, happy and free. A crocodile swims by, and the two become unlikely friends. Every day the monkey tosses sweet rose-apples down to the crocodile, who eats some and carries the rest home to his wife.

The fruit is so delicious that the crocodile's wife grows greedy. She reasons: if the fruit is this sweet, the heart of the monkey who eats it all day must be sweeter still. She demands her husband bring her the monkey's heart.

The crocodile is torn — this is his friend — but he gives in. He goes to the monkey and says, 'My wife has prepared a great feast in your honor. Come, ride on my back across the river to our home.'

Here is the moment. The monkey is flattered. A feast, in his honor? He climbs onto the crocodile's back, glowing with the attention, and they set off across the deep water.

Halfway across, with the monkey now helpless far from his tree, the crocodile feels guilty and blurts out the truth: 'I'm sorry, friend. My wife wants to eat your heart. That's why I brought you.'

The monkey's stomach drops. He is in the middle of a river, on the back of a predator, lured there by nothing but flattery and the promise of being celebrated. The praise that felt so good has carried him straight into the jaws of death.

But the monkey is quick. He hides his fear and says lightly, 'Oh, why didn't you tell me sooner? We monkeys keep our hearts hanging in the tree for safekeeping. I left mine back home. Take me back and I'll happily fetch it for you.'

The foolish crocodile, relieved, swims him back to shore. The instant they reach the tree, the monkey leaps up into the branches — safe, and far wiser.

'You fool,' he calls down. 'No one keeps their heart in a tree. And no one should ride into deep water on the back of someone who feeds them flattery.'

The fruit, the friendship, the feast 'in his honor' — all of it nearly cost the monkey his life. What saved him wasn't avoiding praise. It was waking up, mid-river, to what the praise was actually for.

sections.title: The Contrast
sections.content:
The Roman Stoic philosopher Epictetus, who was born a slave and became one of history's great teachers, put it bluntly: "If you wish to be loved, do not be enslaved by the desire to be loved."

He taught that the moment your peace depends on others' approval, you've handed them the controls. Praise feels like a gift, but a gift you need becomes a leash. Anyone who can grant the applause you crave can also withhold it — and now they own you. The free person can enjoy praise the way you enjoy good weather: pleasant when it comes, but never something you'd reshape your life to chase.

Praise is a fine reward and a terrible master.

When approval becomes the fuel your identity runs on, you stop steering by 'what's true' and start steering by 'what gets applause' — and whoever controls the applause now controls you.

sections.title: Why It Happens
sections.content:
Praise hits the same reward circuitry as food, money, and other pleasures — it releases dopamine. And dopamine doesn't just feel good; it teaches. It tags whatever you just did as 'do that again.' So praise literally trains your behavior, often without your consent.

The addiction part comes from a quirk called intermittent reinforcement. If praise came every single time, it would lose its power. But it doesn't — it's unpredictable. Sometimes the work gets celebrated, sometimes it's ignored. That randomness is the exact pattern that makes slot machines and social media so compulsive. Unpredictable rewards are far more addictive than reliable ones, because the brain keeps pulling the lever hoping this time is the hit.

Underneath sits a deeper vulnerability: the need for external validation. If your sense of being worthy was never quite secured from the inside — if it always depended on someone else's nod — then praise isn't a bonus, it's oxygen. And anything you need to breathe, you'll do almost anything to get.

The felt experience is the tell. Healthy satisfaction is quiet and self-contained: 'I did good work, I know it.' The addiction feels different — anxious, hungry, eyes scanning the room for a reaction. When the doing isn't enough without the seeing, the hook is already in.

sections.title: The Trap
sections.content:
The overcorrection is its own trap: the person who decides praise is hollow, refuses all of it, and wears their indifference to others' opinions as a badge. But total imperviousness to feedback isn't strength — it's just a different deafness. Praise carries real information. It tells you what's landing, what's valued, where your effort connects with people. Refuse it entirely and you cut yourself off from a genuine signal.

The goal isn't to stop feeling good when praised. It's to keep your worth and your direction anchored inside, so that praise informs you without governing you. Enjoy the applause. Just don't let it write the script.

sections.title: The Move
sections.content:
1. Define 'good' before you're judged. Before you start the work, write down what success actually looks like — by your standard. Now when praise comes, you can check it against your own scorecard instead of letting it become the scorecard.

2. Separate the signal from the supply. When praised, take the information ('this resonated') and set down the craving ('I need more of this to feel okay'). Ask: would I still be proud of this work if no one had noticed? If yes, you're anchored.

3. Do deliberately unseen work. Once a week, do something valuable that no one will praise — the unglamorous fix, the quiet help. It rebuilds the muscle of acting from 'this matters' rather than 'this gets seen.'

4. Notice who holds the leash. Make a list of whose approval you find yourself working for. Just naming them weakens their grip — you can't be unconsciously steered by something you've made conscious.

5. Sit with one unanswered moment. Next time you do something good and feel the itch for acknowledgment that doesn't come, don't fill it. Just feel the discomfort and let it pass. Each time you do, the addiction loses a little of its power over you.
